opp.name: Crusoe_Intake/Source/Eproc/SRPM_June'26
opp.next_step: 6/25 Steve Ovadje : Schedule discovery call with solutions consultant on 7/1 '26 4pm Central
opp.ais_why:
• At Crusoe there was 'notable interest in the platform's capabilities, particularly its AI-driven features that streamline procurement processes,' including a Slack-integrated intake/orchestration need and 'conversational' features.
• The Agentic-platform-discussed flag is set to Yes and current tool Coupa is described as 'convoluted, manual, and not scalable,' motivating an AI-forward alternative.
• Budget is not discussed and the buyer (Ben) is evaluator without confirmed authority, so the clear AI interest is not yet backed by commercial commitment.

sources: Discovery call 25 June 2026 (Ben Slaugh, Business Relationship Management)
Next Step 1 July 2026 (solutions-consultant discovery call scheduled)
metrics.narrative: On the 25 June 2026 discovery call, the buyer (Ben Slaugh, Business Relationship Management, Evaluator role) articulated dissatisfaction with Coupa but did not quantify the business impact in terms of cost, cycle time, error rate, or savings opportunity. The pain is qualitative ('dissatisfaction with Coupa') without a dollar figure, headcount impact, or ROI target attached. When the rep probed budget ('Do you know what your budget is?'), the buyer did not provide a substantive answer, signaling that quantified business value and budget allocation have not yet been scoped. For a deal 7 days old in Qualified stage this is normal—metrics discovery is the purpose of the upcoming solutions-consultant call scheduled for 1 July. The business problem exists (Coupa dissatisfaction) but the quantified case does not yet. Next move: use the 1 July discovery call to extract quantified pain (spend under management, process cycle time, error/rework cost, or headcount tied to manual work) and confirm the financial impact that justifies a $150k platform investment.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Ben Slaugh (BRM, Evaluator), Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement)
Discovery call 25 June 2026 (Ben Slaugh engaged, no advocacy evidence)
Next Step 1 July 2026
champion.narrative: No confirmed champion as of 3 July 2026. Ben Slaugh (Business Relationship Management, Evaluator role) is the primary contact and initiated the 25 June discovery call, but his role is Evaluator, not Champion. A champion requires evidence of advocacy—introducing us to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker, sharing internal decision dynamics, pushing back on competitors on our behalf, or driving the internal agenda toward Zycus. Ben has not yet demonstrated any of these behaviors; he is an engaged evaluator exploring alternatives to Coupa, but advocacy is unproven. Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement, Corporate Services) is the second contact but has not appeared on a call yet, so his stance is unknown. For a deal 7 days old in early discovery, this is expected—champion development typically requires multiple touchpoints and a demonstrated willingness to advocate internally. The status is 'developing' in the sense that Ben is engaged and the relationship is live, but advocacy evidence is absent. Next move: on the 1 July call, assess Ben's willingness to advocate by asking him to facilitate introductions to the budget approver and decision maker, and by probing whether he has a preference for Zycus over Coupa or other alternatives. If he makes introductions or shares internal dynamics, champion development is progressing; if he remains neutral or passive, we need to find another internal advocate.

sources: Salesforce Competitors__c: Coupa
Discovery call 25 June 2026 (Ben Slaugh, dissatisfaction with Coupa)
Next Step 1 July 2026 ('demonstrate workflow configurability and Merlin Intake')
competition.narrative: Coupa is the confirmed incumbent competitor, logged in Salesforce Competitors__c and discussed on the 25 June 2026 discovery call. Ben Slaugh expressed dissatisfaction with Coupa, which is the opening wedge—Coupa's pain points (inferred: lack of workflow configurability, weak AI/intake capabilities, rigidity) are the buyer's stated reason for exploring alternatives. However, switching costs (data migration, user retraining, workflow reconfiguration) are real, and the buyer has not yet committed to a platform migration. The risk is that the buyer decides to tolerate Coupa's pain rather than undertake a replacement project. How we win: position Zycus as the configurable, AI-forward alternative that solves the specific Coupa gaps the buyer articulated (workflow configurability and Merlin Intake automation), and make the migration path and switching-cost mitigation a centerpiece of the demo and commercials. Emphasize Merlin Intake's ability to automate and streamline the intake process that Coupa handles poorly, and offer a phased implementation approach to reduce switching risk. No other competitor has been named as of 3 July 2026, suggesting this may be a Coupa-replacement evaluation rather than a broad RFP, but that hypothesis is unconfirmed and should be tested on the 1 July call.

sources: Discovery call 25 June 2026 (Ben Slaugh, dissatisfaction with Coupa)
Next Step 1 July 2026 ('demonstrate workflow configurability and Merlin Intake')
OpportunityContactRole: Ben Slaugh (BRM, Evaluator), Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement)
identify_pain.narrative: On the 25 June 2026 discovery call, Ben Slaugh (Business Relationship Management, Evaluator) expressed dissatisfaction with Coupa, the incumbent platform. The specific operational or financial pain was not quantified (no cost figure, cycle time, error rate, or headcount impact cited), but the qualitative pain is confirmed: the buyer is unhappy with Coupa and is exploring alternatives. The pain owner is Ben Slaugh in his BRM capacity, and Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement, Corporate Services) is also a contact, suggesting that procurement operations and vendor management are the pain areas. The pain is inferred to be related to workflow rigidity, lack of configurability, or gaps in Coupa's intake and sourcing capabilities—why else would the buyer specifically ask to see 'workflow configurability and Merlin Intake' demonstrated. However, without a verbatim quote of the pain articulated or a quantified impact statement, the pain remains qualitative and unconfirmed at the financial level. Next move: on the 1 July solutions-consultant call, ask Ben or Mohammad to describe the specific breakdowns or inefficiencies in Coupa that are driving this evaluation (e.g., 'What is Coupa not doing today that you need it to do?' or 'What is the cost—time, money, errors—of the current workarounds?'), and capture a quantified pain statement.

sources: No Salesforce field or call evidence on contracting, legal, or implementation mechanics as of 3 July 2026
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics (MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec, ERP integration) are unknown. No discussion of paper process, legal review, or implementation partner involvement has been captured. For a deal 7 days old in Qualified stage with a close date 150 days out, this is expected—paper-process discovery typically happens in later stages (Vendor Selected, Negotiation, Contract In Progress). The gap is genuine: we do not know whether Crusoe has a standard MSA on file, what their legal review timeline looks like, whether InfoSec or IT security review is required for a SaaS procurement platform, or whether an SI or implementation partner is involved. The evidence provides no signal on paper process. Next move: once the deal progresses past discovery and demo into commercials (likely 60–90 days from now), ask the buyer to walk through their contracting and legal review process, including typical timelines, security review requirements, and any partner or SI involvement in implementation scoping.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Ben Slaugh (Evaluator), Mohammad Rasheed (no explicit role logged)
Discovery call 25 June 2026 (budget probe, no substantive answer)
Next Step 1 July 2026
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified or engaged as of 3 July 2026. The two contacts on record—Ben Slaugh (Business Relationship Management, Evaluator) and Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement, Corporate Services)—are operational and mid-level roles without commercial approval authority for a $150k enterprise SaaS platform decision. Budget was probed on the 25 June call ('Do you know what your budget is?') and the buyer did not provide a substantive answer, indicating that budget authority and allocation are unknown. For a deal 7 days old in Qualified stage, the EB has not yet surfaced, which is expected—enterprise deals often do not engage the CFO or VP-level sponsor until after initial discovery and scoping. However, the gap is real: we do not know who controls the commercial decision, whether budget is allocated, or what approval chain this deal must navigate. The evidence shows mid-level procurement and BRM engagement, but no C-level or VP-level sponsor with commercial authority. Next move: use the 1 July solutions-consultant call to ask Ben or Mohammad directly who will approve the budget and sign the contract, and request an introduction to that person within 14 days.

sources: Discovery call 25 June 2026 (Ben Slaugh)
Next Step 1 July 2026 (solutions-consultant call scheduled)
No documented process in Salesforce custom fields
decision_process.narrative: The decision process is unknown. The call history shows only one discovery call (25 June 2026) with Ben Slaugh (BRM, Evaluator role), and a solutions-consultant call scheduled for 1 July. No multi-stage evaluation timeline, approval chain, or decision committee composition has been documented. The buyer did not answer the budget question substantively on 25 June, and no mention of procurement stages (RFI, RFP, shortlist, vendor selection, contracting) or approval requirements has been captured. For a deal 7 days old in Qualified stage, this is expected—process discovery is the job of the upcoming calls. The evidence shows that the buyer is in early discovery mode (evaluating Coupa dissatisfaction, exploring alternatives), but the formal process the buyer will run (single-vendor evaluation vs competitive RFP, internal approval gates, timeline) is not yet known. Next move: on the 1 July call, ask Ben or Mohammad to describe the end-to-end evaluation and approval process, including who is involved at each stage, what gates exist (technical review, security review, legal, finance approval), and what timeline they are working toward.

sources: Next Step 1 July 2026 ('demonstrate workflow configurability and Merlin Intake')
Salesforce Products: iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake
Discovery call 25 June 2026
decision_criteria.narrative: The 25 June discovery call and the Next Step entry indicate that the buyer wants to see 'workflow configurability and Merlin Intake' demonstrated, signaling that configurability (ability to tailor workflows to Crusoe's procurement processes) and AI-driven intake automation are two evaluation criteria. The product scope logged in Salesforce—iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake—suggests a broad SRM + P2P + AI intake evaluation, but the formal decision criteria (scoring rubric, must-haves vs nice-to-haves, evaluation timeline, RFP structure) have not been documented. For a 7-day-old deal in early discovery, this is expected—criteria formalization typically happens after discovery and demo rounds, when the buyer moves to RFI/RFP or shortlisting. The partial signal is that configurability and Merlin Intake are buyer-stated priorities; the gap is that we do not know the full evaluation framework, the scoring weights, or the decision committee's composition. Next move: on the 1 July call, ask Ben or Mohammad to walk through the evaluation process step-by-step, including criteria, scorecard, timeline, and decision committee members.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.
blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.full: Losing on capability fit — AIS fields (AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c) are not populated in Salesforce. However, the buyer explicitly requested a demonstration of Merlin Intake (AI-driven intake automation) on the 25 June call, which is a direct AI appetite signal. Tier inferred: AI Curious (buyer is exploring AI capabilities but has not articulated a quantified AI-driven ROI case or strategic AI mandate). The explicit Merlin Intake request indicates interest, not resistance. Next move: on the 1 July call, probe the buyer's AI maturity (are they piloting AI in other areas? is there a C-level AI mandate?) and position Merlin Intake as the automation unlock for their intake-process pain.

The evidence shows mid-level procurement and BRM engagement, but no C-level or VP-level sponsor with commercial authority. The buyer's explicit ask to see Merlin Intake suggests AI Curious or AI Hungry positioning is viable. No evidence yet that the agentic/I2O narrative has been presented. Next move: on the 1 July solutions-consultant call, lead with Merlin Intake as the centerpiece and probe the buyer's AI maturity and appetite (are they exploring AI-driven automation across procurement, or is Merlin Intake a nice-to-have?).

cro_panel.header: Crusoe Energy Systems — Qualified · $150K · closes 30 Nov

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 0: no break-risk was observed in the swept evidence. Coverage is thin, so read this as 'nothing seen yet,' not 'nothing there.'
commentary.win_position: Win 19 is carried by momentum 61 vs stage-expected 50; champion strength +0.30 (weight 15). Offsetting it: differentiation strength -0.40 (weight 20).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 61 (moving forward): Buyer actively engaged this sweep (live pulse + buyer calls). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 4 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, stage integrity, commercial, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment 37 reflects real customer investment: Customer owns 1 open commitment(s); Customer shared internal process / commitments.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 18 is anchored on the stage win (19), nudged by commitment 37 and momentum 61, and dragged by risk 0. Read is Partial Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

contributions.evidence: Buyer actively engaged this sweep (live pulse + buyer calls).
Next Step carries 2 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

The explicit Merlin Intake request indicates interest, not resistance. items.trigger: Economic Buyer not identified; budget authority unknown; budget probe on 25 June call yielded no substantive answer
items.expected_effect: Map the Economic Buyer and open the commercial conversation. Reduces single-thread risk and accelerates qualification. If the EB is not identified or introduced within 14 days, the deal is at risk of stalling in discovery without commercial traction.

items.action: On the 1 July call, extract a quantified pain statement from Ben or Mohammad: ask 'What is the cost—time, money, errors—of the current Coupa workarounds or breakdowns?' and 'What financial or operational impact would solving this problem have for Crusoe?' Capture a specific cost figure, cycle-time reduction, headcount impact, or ROI target.
items.trigger: Buyer expressed dissatisfaction with Coupa but did not quantify business impact on 25 June call
items.expected_effect: Build the quantified business case that justifies the $150k investment and supports forecast defensibility. Without quantified pain, the deal lacks a financial anchor and is vulnerable to deprioritization or budget cuts.

items.action: On the 1 July call, multi-thread by asking Ben or Mohammad to invite the Decision Maker, the person who will evaluate and select the vendor, to the next demo or workshop. If Mohammad Rasheed (Director of Procurement) has not yet joined a call, request his attendance on the next session within 7 days.
items.trigger: Single-thread risk: only one call (25 June) with Ben Slaugh (Evaluator role); Mohammad Rasheed is a contact but has not appeared on a call
items.expected_effect: Expand the stakeholder map and reduce single-thread risk. Multi-threading is critical for enterprise deals; relying on one Evaluator-level contact exposes the deal to risk if Ben deprioritizes or leaves.

items.action: On the 1 July call, ask Ben or Mohammad to walk through the end-to-end evaluation and approval process: 'What steps will Crusoe take between now and a vendor selection decision? Who is involved at each stage (technical review, security review, legal, finance approval)? What timeline are you working toward?' Capture the process map, decision committee, and key milestones.
items.trigger: Decision process (evaluation timeline, approval chain, RFI/RFP structure) not documented as of 3 July 2026
items.expected_effect: Map the decision process and timeline so the deal team can align milestones and forecast progression accurately. Without a process map, the 30 Nov 2026 close date is a guess, not a plan.

items.action: On the 1 July call, lead the demo with Merlin Intake as the centerpiece and probe the buyer's AI maturity: 'Are you exploring AI-driven automation in other areas of procurement or the business? Is there a C-level AI mandate or initiative at Crusoe?' Position Merlin Intake as the automation unlock for their intake-process pain and the wedge against Coupa's lack of AI capabilities.
items.trigger: Buyer explicitly requested Merlin Intake demo on 25 June call, indicating AI appetite; AIS fields not populated, tier unknown
items.expected_effect: Anchor the AI narrative and differentiate Zycus from Coupa on AI/automation capabilities. If the buyer has a strategic AI mandate, Merlin Intake becomes the wedge; if AI is a nice-to-have, reposition around configurability and operational pain.

north_star_verdict.math: Deal created 26 June 2026, swept 3 July 2026 (7 days old). Stage: Qualified. Days to close: 150 (close 30 Nov 2026). Time in Qualified: 7 days. No qualified-date logged; using created_date as proxy. Forward slip: none (too new). Pace required: enterprise motion for this stage typically allows 60–90 days for discovery, demos, and RFI/RFP positioning before moving to Shortlisted. At 7 days in and 150 days remaining, the deal is early and on schedule. Rep-initiated outreach dated 1 July 2026 to schedule discovery call with solutions consultant; awaiting buyer reply (not yet a two-way buyer touch post-creation call on 25 June). LastActivityDate 9 July 2026 is 6 days in the future from today (3 July), indicating a scheduled future event—likely the planned discovery call. No slippage, no overdue commitments.

north_star_verdict.headline: Brand-new deal (7 days old) in early Qualified stage with 150 days to close; rep-initiated solutions-consultant discovery call scheduled for 1 July awaiting buyer reply. Highly premature for forecast evaluation—this is discovery-stage activity. Stage and timeline are aligned with enterprise motion expectations.

flags: Single-thread risk: Ben Slaugh is the only engaged contact (Evaluator role, no budget authority). The account has senior procurement leaders (VP of Procurement, Director of Procurement) but none are linked to this opportunity. Use the 01 July demo to request introductions to the broader procurement and IT stakeholders who will drive the evaluation and approval.

Economic Buyer access is the immediate unlock: the buyer expressed clear pain and urgency on 25 June but budget authority has not been mapped. Ask Ben Slaugh on the 01 July call: 'Who will need to approve this commercially, and can we include them in the next conversation?' The VP of Procurement and Director of Procurement are the likely EB candidates—surface the path to them.

Quantify the pain to build urgency: the buyer articulated qualitative dissatisfaction with Coupa ('bolt-ons', 'not integrated') but no quantified impact. On the 01 July demo, probe: 'What does the current Coupa friction cost you in cycle time, user support, or process inefficiency? If we could cut that by X%, what would that be worth?' Anchor the value case early to justify the migration effort.

Single-thread risk: only one contact (Ben Slaugh, Evaluator-level) engaged; no senior procurement, finance, or IT stakeholders mapped or linked to the opportunity. Request introductions on 01 July call.
Economic Buyer not identified: budget authority unknown, no CPO/VP Procurement/CFO engaged. Ask Ben who controls the budget and request an introduction by mid-July.
No quantified business case: pain is qualitative ('frustrated with Coupa') but not translated into cost, cycle time, or ROI metrics. Use 01 July discovery to capture quantified impact.

Champion status unclear: Ben Slaugh is a friendly Evaluator but has not shown advocacy, access to power, or internal influence. Assess champion potential on 01 July; if passive, escalate to find a senior sponsor.
Competitive intel incomplete: unknown whether other vendors (Ariba, Jaggaer, Ivalua, GEP) are in the evaluation. Ask Ben directly on 01 July.

best_practice_check.summary: Brand-new deal (7 days old) in early discovery. Single-thread risk (one call, one active contact, Ben Slaugh Evaluator role). Economic Buyer not identified. Budget unknown. Champion not confirmed (Ben is engaged evaluator, not advocate). These are expected gaps for a 7-day-old Qualified-stage deal; the upcoming 1 July solutions-consultant call is the opportunity to multi-thread, map the EB, and qualify budget.

competitive_position.summary: Coupa is the incumbent and sole named competitor. Buyer expressed dissatisfaction with Coupa on 25 June call, which is the wedge. Switching costs (data migration, user retraining, workflow reconfiguration) are real, and the buyer has not yet committed to a platform migration. No other competitor named, suggesting a Coupa-replacement evaluation rather than a broad RFP, but that hypothesis is unconfirmed.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: Buyer expressed dissatisfaction with Coupa (Ben Slaugh, 25 June 2026 discovery call)

items.quote: Schedule discovery call with solutions consultant on 7/1 '26 4pm Central (Next Step 1 July 2026)
items.requirement: Solutions-consultant discovery call to demonstrate workflow configurability and Merlin Intake

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin Intake is in product scope and was explicitly requested by the buyer on 25 June ('demonstrate Merlin Intake'), indicating AI appetite. However, AIS (AI excitement) fields are not populated in Salesforce, so the tier and score are unknown. The buyer's explicit ask to see Merlin Intake suggests AI Curious or AI Hungry positioning is viable. No evidence yet that the agentic/I2O narrative has been presented. Next move: on the 1 July solutions-consultant call, lead with Merlin Intake as the centerpiece and probe the buyer's AI maturity and appetite (are they exploring AI-driven automation across procurement, or is Merlin Intake a nice-to-have?).

items.quote: Buyer requested demonstration of 'workflow configurability' (Next Step 1 July 2026)
Buyer requested demonstration of 'Merlin Intake' (Next Step 1 July 2026); Merlin Intake is in product scope
items.source: Next Step 1 July 2026, Salesforce Products: Merlin Intake

items.quote: Buyer expressed dissatisfaction with Coupa (Ben Slaugh, 25 June 2026 discovery call)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Buyer priorities: workflow configurability and Merlin Intake (AI-driven intake automation). Product scope logged (iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake) aligns with buyer's stated needs. Configurability and AI intake are Zycus strengths; position is aligned. Formal evaluation criteria, scoring rubric, and must-haves vs nice-to-haves not yet documented.

hard.ais_why:
• At Crusoe there was 'notable interest in the platform's capabilities, particularly its AI-driven features that streamline procurement processes,' including a Slack-integrated intake/orchestration need and 'conversational' features.
• The Agentic-platform-discussed flag is set to Yes and current tool Coupa is described as 'convoluted, manual, and not scalable,' motivating an AI-forward alternative.
• Budget is not discussed and the buyer (Ben) is evaluator without confirmed authority, so the clear AI interest is not yet backed by commercial commitment.

deltas.subject: champion

Single-thread risk: Ben Slaugh is the only engaged contact (Evaluator role, no budget authority). The account has senior procurement leaders (VP of Procurement, Director of Procurement) but none are linked to this opportunity. Use the 01 July demo to request introductions to the broader procurement and IT stakeholders who will drive the evaluation and approval.

Economic Buyer access is the immediate unlock: the buyer expressed clear pain and urgency on 25 June but budget authority has not been mapped. Ask Ben Slaugh on the 01 July call: 'Who will need to approve this commercially, and can we include them in the next conversation?' The VP of Procurement and Director of Procurement are the likely EB candidates—surface the path to them.

Quantify the pain to build urgency: the buyer articulated qualitative dissatisfaction with Coupa ('bolt-ons', 'not integrated') but no quantified impact. On the 01 July demo, probe: 'What does the current Coupa friction cost you in cycle time, user support, or process inefficiency? If we could cut that by X%, what would that be worth?' Anchor the value case early to justify the migration effort.

value.flag: Quantify the pain to build urgency: the buyer articulated qualitative dissatisfaction with Coupa ('bolt-ons', 'not integrated') but no quantified impact. Anchor the value case early to justify the migration effort.
